bytes.topic: Container Fundamentals — images vs containers, layers, copy-on-write filesystem
bytes.title: Docker Images vs Containers — Layers, Copy-on-Write, and What Actually Lives on Disk
bytes.body: A Docker image is a read-only stack of layers, each produced by a Dockerfile instruction. Run `docker image inspect nginx:alpine` and you'll see `RootFS.Layers` — each a SHA256 content-addressed tarball stored under `/var/lib/docker/overlay2/`. When you run `docker run`, Docker creates a thin writable layer on top via OverlayFS (the default storage driver). That writable layer is the container. The image layers are never modified — copy-on-write (CoW) kicks in the moment a container writes to a file that exists in a lower layer: OverlayFS copies the file up to the writable layer first, then modifies it. This is why two containers sharing the same image consume almost no extra disk for reads. It also explains why writing large files inside a container is slower than on the host — the CoW copy-up has overhead. Keep image layers small and ordered by change frequency (deps before app code) to maximize layer cache hits during builds. Use `docker diff <container>` to inspect exactly which files a running container has mutated.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Inspect layers of an image
docker image inspect nginx:alpine --format '{{json .RootFS.Layers}}' | jq .

# Run two containers from the same image — shared read layers, isolated write layers
docker run -d --name c1 nginx:alpine
docker run -d --name c2 nginx:alpine

# See CoW in action: mutate a file in c1 only
docker exec c1 sh -c 'echo hello > /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html'

# diff shows only c1's writable layer changed
docker diff c1   # C /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html
docker diff c2   # (empty — image layer untouched)

# Check disk usage breakdown: image layers vs container writable layers
docker system df -v

bytes.topic: Dockerfile Best Practices — layer caching, multi-stage builds, minimal base images
bytes.title: Docker Dockerfile Best Practices — Layer Caching, Multi-Stage Builds, and Minimal Base Images
bytes.body:
Every RUN, COPY, and ADD instruction creates a new layer. Docker invalidates cache from the first changed layer downward — so order matters. Put infrequently changing instructions (installing OS deps) before frequently changing ones (copying source code). Never COPY . . before installing dependencies; copy only the lockfile first, run the install, then copy source.

Multi-stage builds cut final image size dramatically. Use a fat builder stage with full toolchains, then COPY --from=builder only the compiled artifact into a minimal runtime stage. A Go binary that builds in a 1.22-golang image ships in scratch. A Node app built in node:20 can run in node:20-alpine or even a distroless image.

For base images, prefer -alpine or Google's distroless over full Debian/Ubuntu. Alpine images are ~5 MB vs ~80 MB for debian:slim. Distroless images contain no shell, package manager, or utilities — shrinking the attack surface to near zero. Pin exact digest tags (FROM node:20.11.0-alpine3.19@sha256:...) in production to prevent silent upstream mutations.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Stage 1: build
FROM node:20.11.0-alpine3.19 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: runtime (distroless)
FROM gcr.io/distroless/nodejs20-debian12
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
CMD ["dist/server.js"]

bytes.topic: Image Security — CVE scanning, non-root user, distroless images
bytes.title: Docker Image Security — CVE Scanning, Non-Root Users, and Distroless Builds
bytes.body:
Every layer you ADD to a Docker image is an attack surface. Start by scanning with `docker scout cves <image>` (or `trivy image <image>`) to surface CVEs before they hit production — pipe this into CI to fail builds on HIGH/CRITICAL findings.

Running containers as root is the most common misconfiguration. Add a dedicated user in your Dockerfile: `RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app`, then `USER app` before `CMD`. This prevents privilege escalation if the container is compromised.

Distroless images (e.g., `gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12`) ship with no shell, no package manager, and no OS utilities — dramatically shrinking the CVE surface. Pair them with multi-stage builds: compile in a full image, copy only the binary into distroless. The final image has nothing an attacker can pivot from. For Go or Rust static binaries, `FROM scratch` goes even further — literally zero base layer. Combine all three: scan in CI, drop root at runtime, ship distroless.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Stage 1: build
FROM golang:1.23-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o server .

# Stage 2: distroless runtime
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
# nonroot tag sets USER 65532 automatically
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]

bytes.topic: Kubernetes Architecture — control plane vs worker nodes, etcd, API server
bytes.title: Docker to Kubernetes — Control Plane, etcd, and How Your Containers Actually Get Scheduled
bytes.body:
Docker Desktop ships a single-node Kubernetes cluster where control plane and worker responsibilities collapse onto one machine — making it ideal for observing the full architecture locally.

The control plane has three critical components. The API server is the sole entry point for all cluster operations — every `kubectl` command hits it over HTTPS, authenticated and authorized before anything happens. etcd is a distributed key-value store that holds the entire cluster state: pod specs, node registrations, ConfigMaps, Secrets. Nothing persists anywhere else. The scheduler watches etcd for unscheduled pods and assigns them to nodes based on resource requests in your manifests.

Worker nodes run the actual workloads. The kubelet agent on each node continuously reconciles pod specs from the API server against local container state. Since Kubernetes 1.24, the dockershim was removed — worker nodes now talk to containerd directly, the same low-level runtime Docker itself has used since v1.11. Your Docker-built OCI images work without modification; only the orchestration layer changed.

The request flow: `kubectl apply` → API server validates and writes to etcd → scheduler assigns a node → kubelet on that node instructs containerd to pull and start your image.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Enable Kubernetes in Docker Desktop: Settings → Kubernetes → Enable Kubernetes
# Then verify control plane components are running:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system

# Check control plane component health (API server, scheduler, etcd)
kubectl get componentstatuses

# Inspect etcd directly — every k8s object lives here as a key
kubectl exec -it etcd-docker-desktop -n kube-system -- \
  etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  get /registry/pods/default --prefix --keys-only

bytes.topic: Pods & Deployments — pod lifecycle, replica sets, deployment strategies
bytes.title: Docker Swarm — Replica Sets, Service Lifecycle, and Rolling Deployments
bytes.body:
Docker Swarm models deployments as Services, not raw containers. A Service declares desired state — image, replica count, update policy — and the Swarm manager reconciles actual vs. desired continuously. Each replica runs as a Task, which progresses through states: `NEW → PENDING → ASSIGNED → PREPARING → RUNNING → SHUTDOWN → COMPLETE/FAILED`. If a task fails, the manager schedules a replacement on a healthy node automatically.

Replica sets are implicit: set `--replicas 3` and Swarm ensures exactly 3 tasks run at all times. For zero-downtime deployments, configure `--update-parallelism` (tasks updated simultaneously) and `--update-delay` (wait between batches). Setting `--update-failure-action rollback` triggers automatic rollback if the new image fails its healthcheck. You can also trigger manual rollback with `docker service rollback`. Combine with `--health-cmd` in your Dockerfile to gate rollouts on real readiness, not just process start — critical for services with slow initialization like JVM apps.
bytes.code_snippet:
docker service create \
  --name api \
  --replicas 4 \
  --update-parallelism 1 \
  --update-delay 15s \
  --update-failure-action rollback \
  --rollback-parallelism 2 \
  --health-cmd "curl -f http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1" \
  --health-interval 10s \
  --health-retries 3 \
  myregistry/api:v2.1

bytes.topic: Services & Networking — ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer, cluster DNS
bytes.title: Docker Networking — Bridge vs Host vs Overlay Networks and Built-in DNS
bytes.body: Docker ships four production-relevant network drivers, each with a distinct routing model. Bridge (default) creates an isolated virtual switch per-daemon; containers get a private subnet (172.17.0.0/16) and reach the host via NAT. Crucially, user-defined bridge networks enable Docker's embedded DNS: containers resolve each other by service name, no `/etc/hosts` hacking needed. Host mode bypasses the network namespace entirely — the container binds directly to the host's NIC, eliminating NAT overhead but sacrificing isolation. Overlay spans multiple Docker daemons (Swarm mode), stitching distributed containers into a single L2 fabric with automatic VXLAN encapsulation. Port publishing (`-p 8080:80`) is your NodePort equivalent: Docker inserts an iptables DNAT rule routing host traffic into the container. For production load-balancing, pair Overlay with Swarm's built-in VIP routing mesh — each service gets a stable virtual IP, and the Swarm load-balances across replicas transparently. On user-defined networks, `docker exec app curl http://db:5432` resolves via 127.0.0.11 (Docker's internal DNS resolver), not broadcast.
bytes.code_snippet:
# docker-compose.yml — explicit network with DNS-based service discovery
services:
  api:
    image: myapp:latest
    ports:
      - "8080:80"        # iptables DNAT → NodePort analogue
    networks:
      - backend

  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    networks:
      - backend           # reachable as 'db:5432' via Docker DNS

networks:
  backend:
    driver: bridge        # swap for 'overlay' in Swarm mode
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: 10.10.0.0/24

bytes.topic: ConfigMaps & Secrets — externalizing config, secrets encryption at rest
bytes.title: Docker Secrets & Configs — Externalizing Config and Encrypting Secrets at Rest in Swarm
bytes.body:
Docker Swarm's native secret and config objects externalize configuration without baking credentials into images or leaking them through environment variables. Secrets are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM inside the Raft consensus store and delivered to authorized tasks via an in-memory tmpfs mount at `/run/secrets/<name>` — they never touch disk on the worker node.

Configs work identically but store plaintext — use them for non-sensitive data like `nginx.conf` or `prometheus.yml`, Docker's equivalent of Kubernetes ConfigMaps. Both are version-immutable: an update means creating a new secret/config object and performing a rolling service update.

Access is declared explicitly per service. A container without `--secret db_password` cannot read the value even when collocated on the same node — a hard isolation boundary that env vars can't offer (env vars leak into child processes, `/proc`, and crash dumps).

For standalone Docker outside Swarm, the encrypted Raft store doesn't exist. Docker Compose's `secrets:` block backed by host files works for local dev, but encryption at rest is your OS's responsibility — pair it with a secrets manager like Vault or AWS SSM Parameter Store for production.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create a secret — encrypted at rest in Swarm's Raft store
echo "s3cr3t-db-pass" | docker secret create db_password -

# Create a config — plaintext, for non-sensitive externalized config
docker config create nginx_conf ./nginx.conf

# Deploy service with secret + config wired in
docker service create \
  --name api \
  --secret db_password \
  --config source=nginx_conf,target=/etc/nginx/nginx.conf \
  myapp:latest

# Secret is readable inside the container at:
# /run/secrets/db_password  (tmpfs, read-only, never hits disk)

bytes.topic: Resource Management — requests vs limits, QoS classes, avoiding OOMKill
bytes.title: Docker Resource Management — Limits, Reservations, and Surviving OOMKill
bytes.body:
Docker's `--memory` flag sets a hard ceiling — breach it and the Linux OOM killer terminates your container instantly. `--memory-reservation` is the soft limit: the kernel may reclaim memory from the container under host pressure without killing it. These map loosely to Kubernetes requests/limits, but operate at the cgroup level directly.

Without explicit `--memory-swap`, Docker allows 2× your memory limit in swap. Set `--memory-swap` equal to `--memory` to disable swap entirely and surface memory leaks immediately rather than masking them.

Three practical QoS tiers emerge: containers with no constraints (BestEffort — OOM-killed first), those with only `--memory` (Burstable — killed when limit is hit), and containers with both `--memory` and `--memory-reservation` set (Guaranteed-like — kernel reclaims before killing).

For CPU, `--cpus` caps absolute core usage. `--cpu-shares` (default 1024) sets relative scheduling weight under contention — a container at 512 shares gets half the CPU of one at 1024 when both are busy.

Monitor live via `docker stats` or read `/sys/fs/cgroup/memory/memory.usage_in_bytes` directly. Never set `--oom-kill-disable` in production — it deflects kills onto other host processes.
bytes.code_snippet:
docker run -d \
  --name api-server \
  --memory="512m" \
  --memory-reservation="384m" \
  --memory-swap="512m" \
  --cpus="1.5" \
  --cpu-shares=1024 \
  my-api:latest

# Verify live resource usage and OOM pressure
docker stats api-server --no-stream

bytes.topic: Health Checks — liveness vs readiness vs startup probes
bytes.title: Docker Health Checks — Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes Explained
bytes.body:
Docker's HEALTHCHECK instruction maps directly to the three probe patterns popularized by Kubernetes — you just have to wire them up intentionally.

**Startup probe** → use `start_period` in your Compose `healthcheck`. During this window, failing checks don't mark the container unhealthy. Critical for JVM apps or DBs that take 30–60s to initialize.

**Liveness probe** → the HEALTHCHECK itself. `CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/healthz || exit 1`. Docker restarts the container (in Swarm) or flags it `unhealthy` (standalone) after `retries` consecutive failures. Keep this check cheap — no DB round-trips.

**Readiness probe** → not native to standalone Docker, but in Compose you achieve it with `depends_on: condition: service_healthy`. Dependent containers won't start until the target passes its HEALTHCHECK. Pair this with a `/ready` endpoint that validates DB connectivity, cache warmth, etc. — distinct from `/healthz`.

Key gotcha: `interval` + `timeout` + `retries` × `start_period` defines your worst-case startup tolerance. Tune these per service, not globally.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Dockerfile
HEALTHCHECK --interval=10s --timeout=3s --start-period=30s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/healthz || exit 1

# docker-compose.yml
services:
  api:
    build: .
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8080/ready"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 30s
  worker:
    depends_on:
      api:
        condition: service_healthy

bytes.topic: Helm — chart structure, values files, templating, release management
bytes.title: Helm for Docker Apps — Chart Structure, Values Files, and Immutable Releases
bytes.body:
Helm packages your Dockerized applications into versioned, reproducible charts. A chart's skeleton — `Chart.yaml` (metadata), `values.yaml` (defaults), and `templates/` (Kubernetes manifests) — maps directly to how Docker images are referenced and deployed.

The `values.yaml` is your Docker image's runtime contract: `image.repository`, `image.tag`, and `image.pullPolicy` are the three fields every chart template reaches for. In `templates/deployment.yaml`, `{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}` renders the exact image string the container runtime will pull.

Release management is where Helm earns its keep. `helm upgrade --install` is idempotent — installs on first run, upgrades thereafter. Pair it with `--atomic` to auto-rollback if your Docker container's readiness probe never fires. In CI, pin to a content-addressed Docker digest rather than a mutable tag to guarantee the exact image layers ship — a mutable `latest` tag is a silent footgun in production.

Use `helm list` to audit active releases and `helm rollback <release> <revision>` to revert. Helm stores each revision's full values, so the Docker image tag that shipped is always recoverable.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Pin to a Docker content digest for immutable releases
DIGEST=$(docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' \
  ghcr.io/myorg/api:3.2.1 | cut -d'@' -f2)

helm upgrade --install api ./chart \
  --namespace production \
  --set image.repository=ghcr.io/myorg/api \
  --set image.tag="${DIGEST}" \
  --set replicaCount=3 \
  --atomic \
  --timeout 5m

# Revert to the previous Docker image revision
helm rollback api 0

bytes.topic: Autoscaling — HPA, VPA, KEDA for event-driven scaling
bytes.title: Docker Container Autoscaling — HPA, VPA, and KEDA for Event-Driven Workloads
bytes.body:
HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) scales Docker container replicas by polling metrics-server every 15s against the `resources.requests` defined in your container spec. Set `minReplicas`/`maxReplicas` and a `averageUtilization` target — Kubernetes adjusts replica count accordingly. Simple, but CPU-driven scaling lags behind bursty workloads.

VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler) right-sizes container `resources.requests` and `resources.limits` based on observed usage. In `Auto` mode it evicts and restarts containers with corrected values. Critical conflict: HPA and VPA fighting over the same CPU metric will cause oscillation — VPA changes `requests`, shifting HPA's utilization denominator. Run VPA in `Initial` mode alongside HPA, or avoid the combination entirely.

KEDA unlocks event-driven scaling for Docker containers with 50+ scalers: Kafka consumer lag, RabbitMQ queue depth, Redis list length, Azure Service Bus message count. It creates a shadow HPA internally but drives it from external event sources. `minReplicaCount: 0` lets idle consumer containers scale to zero completely — impossible with vanilla HPA. For queue-based worker containers, target lag per replica rather than CPU: 10 unprocessed Kafka messages per container replica is far more responsive than any utilization threshold.
bytes.code_snippet:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: kafka-worker-scaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: docker-worker        # Deployment running your container image
  minReplicaCount: 0           # scale-to-zero when queue is empty
  maxReplicaCount: 30
  triggers:
  - type: kafka
    metadata:
      bootstrapServers: kafka-broker:9092
      consumerGroup: worker-group
      topic: job-queue
      lagThreshold: "10"       # 1 replica per 10 unprocessed messages
EOF

bytes.topic: RBAC & Namespaces — multi-tenancy, least privilege in cluster
bytes.title: Docker User Namespaces — Enforcing Least Privilege in Multi-Tenant Clusters
bytes.body: Running multiple tenants on shared Docker infrastructure without user namespace remapping means every `root` inside a container maps to `root` on the host — a container escape becomes a full host compromise. Enable `userns-remap` in `daemon.json` to remap container UIDs/GIDs to a subordinate unprivileged range. With `"userns-remap": "default"`, Docker auto-creates a `dockremap` user and maps UID 0 inside the container to UID 100000+ outside — a kernel-enforced boundary. Layer this with per-tenant bridge networks to enforce network namespace isolation: containers on separate networks cannot communicate even if they share a host. Add `--cap-drop ALL --cap-add <explicit>` per workload to strip capabilities that aren't needed — most app containers only need `NET_BIND_SERVICE`. Never bind-mount shared host paths across tenants; use isolated named volumes instead. Verify the remapping is live: `id` inside the container returns `uid=0`, but `ps aux` on the host shows UID `100000+`. This three-layer model — user namespaces, network namespaces, capability restrictions — gives you meaningful tenant boundaries without Kubernetes overhead.
bytes.code_snippet:
# /etc/docker/daemon.json
# { "userns-remap": "default" }
# systemctl restart docker

# Isolated per-tenant networks
docker network create --driver bridge tenant-a-net
docker network create --driver bridge tenant-b-net

# Least-privilege container for tenant-a
docker run -d --name svc-a \
  --network tenant-a-net \
  --cap-drop ALL --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE \
  --read-only --tmpfs /tmp \
  nginx:alpine

# Verify: root inside != root outside
docker exec svc-a id        # uid=0(root) inside
ps aux | grep nginx          # uid=100000 on host

bytes.topic: Network Policies — pod-to-pod traffic rules, ingress controllers
bytes.title: Docker Network Isolation — Controlling Container-to-Container Traffic and Ingress
bytes.body:
Docker enforces container isolation through named networks backed by Linux bridge devices and iptables chains. Every `docker network create` spins up a new bridge (e.g., `br-a1b2c3d4`) and inserts FORWARD rules controlling cross-network traffic.

By default, containers on the same user-defined bridge network communicate freely via Docker's embedded DNS — containers resolve each other by service name. Containers on different networks cannot reach each other at all without explicit multi-network membership.

The `internal: true` flag on a network inserts an iptables DROP rule on the FORWARD chain for external IPs, blocking all outbound routing. Use this to enforce strict ingress-only topologies: your reverse proxy sits on a public-facing network, your app bridges both, and your database lives exclusively on the internal network — unreachable from any external interface.

For ingress, Traefik's Docker provider reads container labels at runtime and dynamically wires HTTP routing rules without a restart. Only the ingress container exposes host ports — upstream services stay unexposed, communicating purely over Docker's virtual bridge.
bytes.code_snippet:
services:
  traefik:
    image: traefik:v3.0
    command: --providers.docker=true --entrypoints.web.address=:80
    ports:
      - "80:80"
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
    networks: [public]

  api:
    image: myapp-api:latest
    labels:
      - "traefik.http.routers.api.rule=Host(`api.local`)"
      - "traefik.http.services.api.loadbalancer.server.port=8080"
    networks: [public, internal]  # bridges both; only ingress point

  db:
    image: postgres:16
    networks: [internal]           # no host ports; zero external exposure

networks:
  public: {}
  internal:
    internal: true                 # iptables DROP on FORWARD for external IPs

bytes.topic: Rolling Updates & Rollbacks — zero-downtime deployments, rollback strategy
bytes.title: Docker Swarm Rolling Updates — Zero-Downtime Deploys and Instant Rollbacks
bytes.body:
Docker Swarm's service update mechanism handles rolling deployments natively. When you run `docker service update`, Swarm replaces tasks in batches controlled by `--update-parallelism` (tasks updated simultaneously) and `--update-delay` (wait between batches). Set `--update-failure-action rollback` to automatically revert if a task fails its health check within `--update-monitor` window.

Rollback configuration mirrors update config: `--rollback-parallelism` and `--rollback-delay` control the revert cadence. Swarm stores the previous service spec, so `docker service rollback <service>` instantly reverts to the last known-good configuration without re-pulling an image.

For zero-downtime, pair rolling updates with a HEALTHCHECK in your Dockerfile. Swarm won't mark a task healthy until the health check passes — stopping the rollout from advancing if your new container is broken. Use `--update-order start-first` to spin up the new task before killing the old one, eliminating any gap in capacity during the swap.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Deploy with rolling update config and auto-rollback on failure
docker service update \
  --image myapp:v2.1.0 \
  --update-parallelism 1 \
  --update-delay 10s \
  --update-monitor 30s \
  --update-failure-action rollback \
  --update-order start-first \
  --rollback-parallelism 2 \
  --rollback-delay 5s \
  my_service

# Manual rollback to previous spec
docker service rollback my_service

bytes.topic: Podman — rootless containers, daemonless architecture, Docker compatibility
bytes.title: Docker vs Podman — Rootless Containers and a Daemon-Free Alternative
bytes.body:
Podman is a drop-in Docker replacement that eliminates the central dockerd daemon entirely. Each `podman` command forks a container process directly — no long-running root daemon means no single point of failure and a dramatically reduced attack surface.

The killer feature is rootless mode: containers run as your actual UID, so a container escape lands the attacker in an unprivileged user namespace, not on bare metal as root. Docker added rootless support later, but it still requires rootlesskit shims; Podman's rootless is first-class.

Docker compatibility is near-total. Podman reads the same OCI images from any registry, respects Dockerfiles unchanged, and ships a `podman-docker` shim that aliases the `docker` CLI command. `DOCKER_HOST=unix:///run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock` points existing Docker SDK clients at Podman's socket.

Podman Compose handles multi-container workloads via the same `docker-compose.yml` format. For CI pipelines already using `docker build` and `docker push`, switching is usually a one-line change in your runner config — swap the binary, keep everything else.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Install podman-docker shim so existing scripts need zero changes
sudo apt-get install -y podman-docker

# Run rootless — no sudo, no daemon
podman run --rm -p 8080:80 nginx:alpine

# Point Docker SDK / Compose at Podman's rootless socket
export DOCKER_HOST=unix:///run/user/$(id -u)/podman/podman.sock
docker compose up -d          # works unmodified

# Build & push — identical to Docker CLI
podman build -t myapp:latest .
podman push myapp:latest docker.io/myuser/myapp:latest

# Verify no daemon is running
systemctl --user status podman.socket   # socket-activated, not always-on

bytes.topic: Observability in K8s — logs, metrics, traces from pods
bytes.title: Docker in K8s — Wiring Logs, Metrics, and Traces from Your Pods
bytes.body:
When your Docker containers run as K8s pods, observability shifts from `docker logs` and `docker stats` to a three-layer pipeline.

**Logs:** kubelet captures each container's stdout/stderr and writes it to `/var/log/containers/<pod>_<ns>_<container>-<id>.log` as JSON. A Fluent Bit DaemonSet tails those node-level files and forwards to your backend. Critical rule: always log to stdout/stderr in your Dockerfile — never write to files inside the container. K8s log rotation only covers the node-level JSON, not in-container paths.

**Metrics:** cAdvisor is embedded in every kubelet and exposes Docker container metrics — CPU throttling, memory working set, network I/O — at `<node-ip>:10250/metrics/cadvisor`. Add `prometheus.io/scrape: "true"` annotations to pods so Prometheus autodiscovers them via `kubernetes_sd_configs`.

**Traces:** Inject `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` and `OTEL_SERVICE_NAME` as env vars directly in the pod spec. The OTEL Collector runs as a DaemonSet and batches spans to your backend. Use `OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES` to attach Docker image tags and pod names to every trace automatically.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Tail logs kubelet writes from your Docker container's stdout
kubectl logs -f pod/api-server -c api-server --timestamps

# cAdvisor: scrape Docker container CPU throttling directly from kubelet
kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/<node-name>/proxy/metrics/cadvisor \
  | grep 'container_cpu_cfs_throttled_seconds_total{container="api-server"}'

# Verify OTEL Collector is ingesting traces from your pods
kubectl logs -n monitoring deploy/otel-collector | grep 'TracesExporter'

# Check the node-level log file kubelet wrote for a crashed container
kubectl debug node/<node-name> -it --image=busybox -- \
  ls /host/var/log/containers/ | grep api-server
